literal: ease, pleasure, happiness; traditionally analyzed as su- ('good, well') + kha ('axle-hole, hub'), 'having a good axle-hole' — i.e. running smoothly, like a wheel well-fitted to its axle; hence comfort, ease. Opposed to duḥkha ('duḥ-kha', running roughly)
invariant_definition: *Sukha* is ease, pleasure, happiness — the smooth-running counterpart of *duḥkha*. The traditional etymology pictures a wheel whose hub is well-fitted to the axle, so that the term carries from the outset the sense of running well, frictionless ease. Two registers must be kept apart. There is *sukha* as ordinary pleasure — the gratification born of sense-contact, which the *Gītā* analyzes by *guṇa*: pleasure that is *sāttvika* (clear at the end though hard at first), *rājasa* (sweet at first and poison at the end), or *tāmasa* (deluding throughout) (18.37–39). And there is *sukha* in the higher, soteriological register — the unconditioned bliss of the self or of liberation, the 'happiness beyond the senses, grasped by the intellect' that the *Gītā* names *ātyantika sukha* (6.21) and that shades toward *ānanda*. The contemplative traditions are careful that sense-born *sukha*, being dependent on contact and therefore impermanent, is in the long analysis a form of *duḥkha* (the Buddhist *vipariṇāma-dukkhatā*, the suffering inherent in change); true and lasting *sukha* is not the opposite pole of pleasure-and-pain but the peace (*śānti*) that lies beyond the pair, the ease of a self no longer dependent on objects. *Sukha* thus names both what the worldly seek and, transposed, what the liberated are said to attain — but the two are sharply distinguished.

school_framing.shared_core: *Sukha* names **ease and happiness — the smooth-running counterpart of *duḥkha***, ranging from sense-born pleasure to the unconditioned bliss of the liberated self. The schools share the term and its two registers; they differ on whether any lasting *sukha* is to be found in objects at all.
school_framing.register_axes_note: A recurring **difference of aspect** between *sukha* as sense-pleasure (dependent on contact, graded by *guṇa*, and in the long view itself a kind of *duḥkha*) and *sukha* as the unconditioned ease of liberation (beyond the pleasure–pain pair, near to *ānanda* and *śānti*). The contemplative traditions agree that the first is impermanent and the second alone is sought.

per_school.definition: The *Gītā* distinguishes worldly *sukha*, graded threefold by *guṇa* (18.37–39), from the supreme happiness of the disciplined self. Sense-contacts are 'wombs of suffering, with a beginning and an end' from which the wise take no delight (5.22); the happiness worth seeking is the *ātyantika sukha* — beyond the senses, grasped by the steadied intellect — in which one rests unshaken (6.20–22). The pleasure of the *sthitaprajña* is thus not the opposite of pain but the equanimous ease (*prasāda*, *śānti*) of a mind no longer dependent on objects.
primary_loci: Bhagavad-Gītā 5.22 (ye hi saṃsparśa-jā bhogā duḥkha-yonaya eva te)
per_school.register_tag: M+S; mumukṣu (sense-pleasure vs the supreme ease)

per_school.definition: *Sukha* (Pali *sukha*) ranges from pleasant feeling (*sukha-vedanā*) to the refined happiness of the *jhānas*, where it is a constituent factor of absorption before being transcended in the equanimity of the higher attainments. But pleasant feeling, because it is impermanent and changes, is itself caught under *dukkha* through *vipariṇāma-dukkhatā* (the unsatisfactoriness of change). The lasting *sukha* of the path is therefore not pleasant sensation but the peace of *nibbāna*, declared the highest happiness (*nibbānaṃ paramaṃ sukhaṃ*).
primary_loci: the jhāna factors (vitakka, vicāra, pīti, sukha, ekaggatā)
vipariṇāma-dukkhatā (pleasant feeling as a form of dukkha)
per_school.register_tag: Φ+S; reflective reader (sukha as factor and as nibbāna)

textual_history.summary: *Sukha* ('ease, pleasure, happiness') is genuinely Vedic, and its archaic, concrete sense is visible in the Ṛgveda: *su-kha*, 'having a good axle-hole' (su- + *kha*, the nave of a wheel), is said of a chariot that runs smoothly — *sukháṃ rátham*, 'the easy-running car'. From this mechanical image of smooth running grows the abstract 'ease, happiness'. The Upaniṣads pair it with its opposite, treating *sukha* and *duḥkha* (the *priya/apriya*, the pleasant and unpleasant) as the dyad the liberated self transcends. The *Gītā* inherits this dvandva and makes equanimity toward the *sukha-duḥkha* pair the mark of the sage (2.14–15, 2.38, 2.56). Honest summary: Vedic in root and image, Upaniṣadic in its pairing, ethicised in the *Gītā*.

stages.locus: RV 1.120.11 (Aufrecht ed.); cf. RV 1.114.01 etc. (sukhaṁ ratham)
stages.rendering: [Yours is] the soma-drinking, the easy-running car (sukho rathaḥ).
stages.note: The concrete root-sense: *sukha* of a chariot — literally 'good-axle-holed', hence smooth-running. The Aśvins' or the god's car is 'sukha', running easily; the abstract 'happiness' is this image of frictionless motion generalised. (Cf. RV 1.114.01 and the standard formula *sukháṃ rátham*, 'the easy car'.) This is the oldest, physical layer of the word.

stages.sanskrit: aśarīraṃ vāva santaṃ na priyāpriye spṛśataḥ
stages.rendering: The pleasant and the unpleasant (priya-apriye) do not touch one who is truly bodiless.
stages.note: The Upaniṣadic pairing: *sukha* and *duḥkha* appear as the dyad *priya/apriya*, 'pleasant/unpleasant', which 'do not touch' the bodiless (liberated) self. Pleasure and pain are bound to embodiment (dharma-adharma); release from the body is release from the pair. This is the conceptual ground the *Gītā* turns into a discipline of equanimity.

stages.sanskrit: mātrā-sparśās tu kaunteya śītoṣṇa-sukha-duḥkhadāḥ | āgamāpāyino 'nityās tāṃs titikṣasva bhārata ||
stages.rendering: The contacts of the senses, Kaunteya, give cold and heat, pleasure and pain (sukha-duḥkha); they come and go, impermanent — endure them, Bhārata.
stages.note: The *Gītā*'s dvandva: *sukha* and *duḥkha* arise from sense-contact, are impermanent, and are to be borne with equanimity. The same pairing is made the sage's measure at 2.38 ('sukha-duḥkhe same kṛtvā', 'making pleasure and pain equal') and 2.56 ('duḥkheṣv anudvigna-manāḥ sukheṣu vigata-spṛhaḥ', 'unshaken in pains, longing gone in pleasures'). Vedic ease becomes an object of mastery.

translator_note: 'Happiness', 'pleasure', or 'ease' all serve, but the register must be fixed by context: ordinary *sukha* is sense-born and impermanent (and in the deep analysis itself a form of *duḥkha*), whereas the *sukha* of liberation is the unconditioned ease beyond the pleasure–pain pair, close to *ānanda* and *śānti*. The smooth-running etymology (su-kha, against duḥ-kha) is worth keeping alive: the contrast the traditions draw is precisely between a life that runs well and one that runs roughly.
